title: Front Desk Guest Check-in
description: Complete check-in process: from greeting to key handover and upsell.

steps.title: Greeting and first impression
steps.content: Stand up when a guest approaches. Eye contact, smile, time-of-day greeting: 'Good morning/afternoon/evening, welcome to our hotel!'. If the guest has luggage — offer bell service. If the guest looks tired — speak softer and shorter.
steps.key_takeaway: Always stand. A seated front-desk agent says 'I don't care'.

steps.title: Finding the reservation in PMS
steps.content: Ask for the guest's last name. Find the booking in PMS (Opera, Cloudbeds, Mews). Verify: dates, room type, payment method, special requests (early check-in, crib, dietary). If not found — ask for confirmation number or email, never say 'we don't have you'.
steps.key_takeaway: Never say 'we don't have you'. Always 'let's find it — do you have a confirmation?'.

steps.title: Documents and registration
steps.content: Ask for passport (ID for locals). Scan or photocopy. Fill the registration card or confirm in PMS. For foreign guests — legal registration required within 24 hours depending on country. Return documents immediately — don't hold them.
steps.key_takeaway: Passport returned = forgotten. Holding ID = complaint and fine.

steps.title: Upsell: room upgrade attempt
steps.content: Offer an upgrade with reasoning: 'We have a suite available tonight, only $30 more — bigger bathroom and park view'. Don't push. If the guest isn't interested — drop it after the first 'no'. Upsell = +3-5% shift revenue.
steps.key_takeaway: One attempt. Heard 'no' — drop the topic. Pushiness = bad review.

steps.title: Payment and deposit
steps.content: Explain: 'In addition to room charges, we'll hold a $100 deposit for minibar and incidentals — it's released on check-out'. Pre-authorize the card. For cash payment — collect cash deposit with a receipt. Always mention deposit upfront, never 'we'll discuss at check-out'.
steps.key_takeaway: Deposit talk happens before, not after. Surprises at check-out = conflict.

steps.title: Orientation and keys
steps.content: State room number and floor. Explain: Wi-Fi password (or QR), breakfast hours and location, spa/gym access, check-out time (noon). Hand the keycard in a sleeve with the room number. For VIPs — walk them to the elevator. Offer luggage help again.
steps.key_takeaway: 5 things: Wi-Fi, breakfast, spa, check-out, emergency number. No more.

steps.title: 15-minute follow-up
steps.content: 15 minutes after check-in, call the room: 'Is everything alright? Everything working?'. This catches 80% of issues before they become complaints (TV not working, no towels, too cold). Log requests in PMS. This one call = the difference between 8.2 and 8.8 on Booking.
steps.key_takeaway: One call at 15 minutes = +0.6 on Booking. Seriously.

quiz.question: How do you greet a guest?
options: Standing, with smile and time-of-day greeting
quiz.explanation: Standing is the baseline. A seated agent looks indifferent.

quiz.question: If the reservation isn't found, what do you say?
options: 'Let's find it — do you have a confirmation?'
quiz.explanation: Never 'we don't have you' — sounds like rejection and triggers conflict.

quiz.question: When do you mention the deposit?
options: At check-out when we return it
quiz.explanation: Upfront. Surprises = bad reviews. Transparency = trust.

quiz.question: How many upsell attempts?
quiz.explanation: One attempt with reasoning. After 'no' — continue check-in.

quiz.question: What does the 15-minute follow-up call do?
quiz.explanation: One call = +0.6 on Booking on average. Proven in practice.

manager_checklist: Knows 3 ways to find a booking (name, confirmation, email)
Avoids phrases 'we don't have you' / 'system error'
Makes one upsell attempt with reasoning
Makes follow-up call 15 minutes after check-in

kpi.metric: Check-in time from arrival to keys
